title: Exception Handling
subtitle: try-except — no checked exceptions, no throws clause

sections.java: // Checked exceptions force try or throws try { String data = Files.readString(path); } catch (IOException e) { log.error("read failed", e); } finally { cleanup(); }
sections.python: # except instead of catch try: data = path.read_text() except OSError as e: log.error("read failed: %s", e) finally: cleanup()

sections.title: No Checked Exceptions — No throws Clause
sections.content: Every Python exception is unchecked (like Java's RuntimeException). There is no throws declaration and the compiler never forces you to catch anything. This removes a huge amount of boilerplate — but the discipline shifts to you: document what your function can raise, because the signature won't.

sections.java: // Multi-catch try { risky(); } catch (IOException | SQLException e) { handle(e); } catch (Exception e) { fallback(e); }
sections.python: # Tuple of types in one except try: risky() except (OSError, ValueError) as e: handle(e) except Exception as e: fallback(e) else: on_success() # ran if no exception

sections.title: try Has an else Clause Too
sections.content: Python's try can take an else block that runs only if no exception was raised — a clean place for the "happy path follow-up" that shouldn't itself be inside the protected try. Order is: try → except → else → finally. There's no Java equivalent.

sections.java: // Custom exception + raise class PaymentError extends RuntimeException { public PaymentError(String msg) { super(msg); } } throw new PaymentError("declined");
sections.python: # Subclass Exception, raise it class PaymentError(Exception): pass raise PaymentError("declined") # Re-raise with context: raise PaymentError("declined") from err

sections.title: Never Write a Bare except:
sections.content: except: with no type catches everything — including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit, which you almost never want to swallow. Always catch a specific type, or except Exception: at the broadest. And don't catch-and-pass silently; at minimum log it. The raise ... from err form preserves the cause chain like Java's initCause.

sections.java: // try-with-resources auto-closes try (BufferedReader r = Files.newBufferedReader(p)) { return r.readLine(); } // r.close() called automatically
sections.python: # with statement = try-with-resources with open(path) as f: return f.readline() # f.close() called automatically, # even if an exception is raised

sections.java: // LBYL: Look Before You Leap if (map.containsKey(key)) { Value v = map.get(key); use(v); } else { handleMissing(); }
sections.python: # EAFP: Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission try: use(data[key]) except KeyError: handle_missing() # Idiomatic Python: attempt first, handle failure — # and avoid check-then-act race conditions.

sections.question: What's the Python equivalent of Java's try-with-resources for guaranteed cleanup?
options: A finally block is the only option
sections.explanation: The with statement is Python's try-with-resources. Any object implementing the context manager protocol (__enter__/__exit__) — like a file from open() — is cleaned up automatically when the block exits, even on exception. It's cleaner than a manual finally.
